statement.markdown: You are given a list of stocks with their respective prices and corresponding transaction costs. Each stock can be either bought or sold once. Your task is to maximize the total profit. You can't sell a stock before you buy it.
statement.inputFormat: Each line contains two space-separated integers representing the cost of buying a stock and the corresponding selling price.
statement.notes: You can't sell a stock before you buy it and each stock can only be bought or sold once.

examples.explanation: Maximum profit 10 from selling stock 2 for 12

hints.text: Sort transactions based on profit-to-cost ratio

editorial.title: Optimal Stock Transactions
editorial.intuition: The optimal strategy is to first sort the transactions based on the profit-to-cost ratio, which represents the potential return on investment.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would involve iterating over each pair of transactions to find the maximum profit.
editorial.betterSolution: The greedy approach involves sorting the transactions based on profit-to-cost ratio and then selecting the transactions with the highest ratio.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves sorting the transactions based on profit-to-cost ratio and then selecting the transactions with the highest ratio while considering the transaction costs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that the greedy approach always results in the maximum possible profit.
editorial.dryRun: The dry run involves sorting the transactions based on profit-to-cost ratio and then selecting the transactions with the highest ratio.
